abstract: Multimodal generation has long been dominated by text-driven pipelines where language dictates vision but cannot reason or create within it. We challenge this paradigm by asking whether all modalities, including textual descriptions, spatial layouts, and editing instructions, can be unified into a single visual representation. We present FlowInOne, a framework that reformulates multimodal generation as a purely visual flow, converting all inputs into visual prompts and enabling a clean image-in, image-out pipeline governed by a single flow matching model. This vision-centric formulation naturally eliminates cross-modal alignment bottlenecks, noise scheduling, and task-specific architectural branches, unifying text-to-image generation, layout-guided editing, and visual instruction following under one coherent paradigm. To support this, we introduce VisPrompt-5M, a large-scale dataset of 5 million visual prompt pairs spanning diverse tasks including physics-aware force dynamics and trajectory prediction, alongside VP-Bench, a rigorously curated benchmark assessing instruction faithfulness, spatial precision, visual realism, and content consistency. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FlowInOne achieves state-of-the-art performance across all unified generation tasks, surpassing both open-source models and competitive commercial systems, establishing a new foundation for fully vision-centric generative modeling where perception and creation coexist within a single continuous visual space.

citations.fullCitation: Yaron Lipman, Ricky T. Q. Chen, Heli Ben-Hamu, Maximilian Nickel, and Matthew Le. Flow matching for generative modeling. In The Eleventh International Conference on Learning Representations, 2023.
citations.justification: This paper is fundamental as it introduces Flow Matching, the core generative mechanism that FlowInOne is built upon. The authors explicitly position their work as an application of this principle, using it as a deterministic and efficient alternative to diffusion models.
citations.title: Flow matching for generative modeling

citations.fullCitation: Robin Rombach, Andreas Blattmann, Dominik Lorenz, Patrick Esser, and Björn Ommer. High-resolution image synthesis with latent diffusion models. In Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, pages 10684–10695, June 2022.
citations.justification: This work introduced Latent Diffusion Models (LDM), which serve as a major point of comparison for FlowInOne. Critically, the FlowInOne architecture directly incorporates the pre-trained and frozen VAE from LDM to operate within a shared latent space.
citations.title: High-resolution image synthesis with latent diffusion models

citations.fullCitation: Qihao Liu, Xi Yin, Alan Yuille, Andrew Brown, and Mannat Singh. Flowing from words to pixels: A noise-free framework for cross-modality evolution. In Proceedings of the Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition Conference, pages 2755–2765, 2025c.
citations.justification: This citation is highly relevant because the paper explicitly states that the FlowInOne model is based on the CrossFlow framework from this work. The authors initialize their model from pre-trained CrossFlow weights, making it the direct architectural backbone of their implementation.
citations.title: Flowing from words to pixels: A noise-free framework for cross-modality evolution

citations.fullCitation: Tim Brooks, Aleksander Holynski, and Alexei A. Efros. Instructpix2pix: Learning to follow image editing instructions. In Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition, pages 18392–18402, June 2023.
citations.justification: This paper is a seminal work in instruction-guided image editing, which is one of the key tasks that FlowInOne aims to unify and solve. It provides essential context for the problem domain and represents a significant prior approach that FlowInOne's vision-centric paradigm improves upon.
citations.title: Instructpix2pix: Learning to follow image editing instructions

intermediateReport:
## Research Report: FLOWINONE: UNIFYING MULTIMODAL GENERATION AS IMAGE-IN, IMAGE-OUT FLOW MATCHING

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research paper "FLOWINONE: UNIFYING MULTIMODAL GENERATION AS IMAGE-IN, IMAGE-OUT FLOW MATCHING" was authored by Junchao Yi, Rui Zhao, Jiahao Tang, Weixian Lei, Linjie Li, Qisheng Su, Zhengyuan Yang, Lijuan Wang, Xiaofeng Zhu, and Alex Jinpeng Wang.

The affiliations of the authors include:
*   University of Electronic Science and Technology of China
*   Central South University
*   National University of Singapore
*   University of Science and Technology of China
*   Tencent
*   Microsoft

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

Multimodal generation has been largely characterized by paradigms where language serves as the primary conditioning input for visual outputs. Models based on diffusion and transformers, for instance, typically translate textual embeddings into visual content. This approach establishes an asymmetry, as language controls visual generation but the visual modality itself is not inherently structured to reason or generate independently within this framework. This can lead to challenges in unifying tasks such as visual understanding, editing, and generation within a single model architecture.

A trend towards vision-centric models has been emerging, exploring the representation of textual information in pixel space for perception-oriented tasks. These studies suggest that the visual modality possesses sufficient expressiveness to serve as a foundational representation for multimodal understanding, enabling unified perception and alignment. However, the generative potential of such a vision-first formulation has not been extensively explored in the context of large-scale models capable of both reasoning and generating entirely within the visual domain.

The field of generative modeling has seen advancements with techniques like Flow Matching, which aims to learn a continuous transport map between data distributions. Compared to diffusion models that rely on progressive denoising and complex noise schedules, Flow Matching directly models the underlying velocity field of transformation, offering potential benefits in sampling efficiency and optimization stability. By learning visual flows, it presents a deterministic principle that could bridge perception and generation.

FlowInOne positions itself within this evolving landscape by taking a step towards unifying multimodal generation as a purely visual flow. It challenges the text-dominant paradigm by proposing a framework where all input modalities—including textual descriptions, spatial layouts, and editing instructions—are converted into visual representations. This approach contrasts with traditional methods that utilize text encoders and modality-specific conditioning branches, aiming to eliminate cross-modal alignment complexities and provide a coherent "image-in, image-out" pipeline. The work seeks to demonstrate that a single model operating entirely within the visual space can effectively handle diverse generative tasks previously requiring fragmented approaches.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary objectives and motivations guiding the development of FlowInOne are:

*   **Challenging the Text-Dominant Paradigm**: The researchers aim to move beyond the conventional text-driven multimodal generation pipeline, where language primarily dictates visual output. They identify an inherent asymmetry in this paradigm, where language governs vision but vision lacks the capability for autonomous reasoning and generation. This fragmentation is perceived as an obstacle to creating a single coherent model for understanding, editing, and generation.

*   **Unification of Modalities into a Single Visual Representation**: A core objective is to investigate whether all diverse input modalities—such as textual descriptions, spatial layouts, and editing instructions—can be seamlessly integrated into a singular visual representation. This visual unification is intended to streamline the input processing for multimodal generation tasks.

*   **Reformulating Multimodal Generation as Purely Visual Flow**: The work seeks to redefine multimodal generation as an "image-in, image-out" process driven by a purely visual flow. This involves transforming all forms of input (text, layout, instructions) into visual prompts that serve as the initial image state. The model then learns a continuous transport process to evolve this state into the desired target visual output.

*   **Elimination of Cross-Modal Bottlenecks and Task-Specific Architectures**: A significant motivation is to address the architectural and alignment complexities associated with traditional multimodal models. By adopting a vision-centric formulation, FlowInOne intends to eliminate issues such as cross-modal alignment bottlenecks, the necessity for noise scheduling inherent in diffusion models, and the proliferation of task-specific architectural branches for different generative tasks.

*   **Unifying Diverse Generative Tasks**: The framework is designed to unify multiple generative tasks under one coherent paradigm. This includes text-to-image generation, layout-guided image editing, and visual instruction following, aiming to demonstrate that a single flow matching model can effectively manage these varied functionalities.

*   **Development of Supporting Resources**: To enable and rigorously evaluate this unified paradigm, the researchers were motivated to create a large-scale dataset, VisPrompt-5M, consisting of 5 million visual prompt pairs across various tasks. Additionally, they developed VP-Bench, a curated benchmark, to assess model performance against criteria such as instruction faithfulness, spatial precision, visual realism, and content consistency.

In summary, the motivation stems from a desire to simplify, unify, and enhance the robustness of multimodal generative models by moving towards a fully vision-centric approach that leverages the expressiveness of the visual modality for both input instruction and output generation.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

FlowInOne reformulates multimodal generation into a vision-centric "image-in, image-out" paradigm, utilizing a single flow matching model. The methodology encompasses several key components:

**4.1. Preliminaries: Flow Matching**
The framework is built upon Flow Matching (FM), which models generative processes as a continuous transport from a source distribution ($p_0$) to a target distribution ($p_1$) over a time interval $t \in [0, 1]$. Unlike diffusion models, FM directly learns the velocity field ($v_{\theta}$) that governs this transformation. Given a sample pair ($z_0, z_1$), representing the source and target latent states, FM constructs a differentiable probability path $z_t = tz_1 + (1 - (1 - \sigma_{min})t) z_0$. The ground-truth velocity field $v^{\star}_t = z_1 - (1 - \sigma_{min})z_0$ is then used to train the network by minimizing the Mean Squared Error (MSE) between $v_{\theta}(z_t, t)$ and $v^{\star}_t$. For FlowInOne, $z_0$ is the latent state of the unified visual instruction (non-Gaussian), and $z_1$ is the target image latent. Inference involves solving an Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE) from $t=0$ to $t=1$ to evolve the visual instruction latent into the final target image latent.

**4.2. Unified Processing of Text in Visual Semantic Space**
To address the modality gap between discrete language and continuous visual data, FlowInOne renders all textual instructions and visual cues directly onto an image canvas, $I_v$. This process maintains spatial layouts and structural priors.
*   **Visual Encoding**: A Janus-Pro-1B visual encoder, specifically a SigLIP Vision Transformer, extracts patch-level semantic features from $I_v$. An MLP projector then maps these features into a target embedding space, producing $X_{fuse} \in R^{N \times D}$, which encapsulates both textual semantics and visual geometry.
*   **Latent Space Mapping**: To facilitate flow matching, $X_{fuse}$ is mapped to a source latent space $Z_{TI}$ via a text-image Variational Autoencoder (VAE), parameterizing a distribution $q_{\psi}(z | X) = N(\bar{\mu}_{z_0}, \text{diag}(\bar{\sigma}^2_{z_0}))$. Simultaneously, a frozen image VAE encodes the target image $I^{\star}$ into an isomorphic latent $Z_I$. The generative process is then defined as modeling the velocity field that continuously transports $Z_{TI}$ to $Z_I$ within this shared latent space.

**4.3. FlowInOne Architecture**
The core architecture is a DiT variant Transformer block augmented with a **Dual-Path Spatially-Adaptive Modulation** mechanism. This mechanism is designed to balance structural preservation and instruction adherence, dynamically compensating for fine-grained structural features potentially lost during visual encoding.
*   **Self-Attention**: The hidden state $H^{(l)}$ of each Transformer layer is first updated via self-attention to capture global contextual information.
*   **Dual-Path Conditional Branch**:
    *   **Text-to-Image Generation (I_edit = 0)**: For tasks where no external structural prior from a source image needs to be maintained, the cross-attention layer is bypassed. This ensures the generation strictly follows the evolution dictated by text semantics.
    *   **Image Editing (I_edit = 1)**: For tasks involving a source image ($I_{src}$), an Image VAE Encoder maps $I_{src}$ into a latent $z_{src}$, which is reshaped into a reference sequence $S$. A cross-attention mechanism computes a structural increment $\Delta H_{struct}$, where the self-attention enhanced state $\tilde{H}^{(l)}$ acts as the Query and $S$ as the Key-Value pair.
*   **Adaptive Gating Network**: A lightweight network predicts an anisotropic token-level weight vector $\Lambda \in [0, 1]^N$. This network concatenates the current denoising state $\tilde{H}^{(l)}$ with $\Delta H_{struct}$ to learn a sigmoid-activated weighting. $\Lambda$ enables the model to selectively inject source priors, distinguishing between background regions requiring preservation and editing regions requiring reconstruction.
*   **Final Layer Output**: The final output is integrated as $H^{(l)}_{out} = \tilde{H}^{(l)} + I_{edit} \cdot (\Lambda \odot \Delta H_{struct})$, where $I_{edit}$ is a binary task indicator. This mechanism precisely controls the infiltration of the structural manifold, mitigating conflicts and reducing evolution error in complex editing scenarios.

**4.4. Dataset and Benchmark**
*   **VisPrompt-5M Dataset**: A large-scale dataset of approximately 5 million visual prompt pairs. It includes eight distinct data types: class-to-image, text-to-image, text-in-image editing, text bounding box editing, visual marker editing, doodles editing, force understanding, and trajectory understanding. Each sample pairs a visual prompt canvas ($I_v$) with a target image ($I^{\star}$). Construction involves direct rendering, MLLM-guided synthesis (using models like Qwen Image Edit and Qwen3-VL), and manual annotation, followed by automated quality control measures (OCR, MLLM-based consistency checks, diversity-oriented deduplication).
*   **VP-Bench Benchmark**: A rigorously curated benchmark of 1,060 manually filtered image pairs. It assesses model performance across four dimensions: Instruction Faithfulness, Content Consistency, Visual Realism, and Spatial Precision. Evaluation is conducted by large multimodal models (e.g., Gemini 3, GPT 5.2, Qwen3.5) and human evaluators, with a generation deemed successful only if all criteria are met. Quantitative metrics include CLIP-IQA, CLIP Score, Directional CLIP Similarity, and DINOv3 Directional Similarity.

**4.5. Training Details**
FlowInOne (1.2B parameters) is initialized from a CrossFlow framework. It is trained at 256x256 resolution for 240k steps using a balanced WebDataset. The optimization objective combines Flow matching loss ($L_{fm}$), KL divergence loss ($L_{kld}$), and CLIP contrastive loss ($L_{clip}$), with specific scaling weights ($\beta_1$, $\beta_2$). The AdamW optimizer with a cosine decay schedule and linear warmup is used.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

FlowInOne demonstrates a strong performance across unified multimodal generation tasks, as assessed by both automated Vision-Language Models (VLMs) and human evaluators.

**5.1. State-of-the-Art Comparison**
*   **VLM and Human Evaluation Pass Rates**: FlowInOne achieved the highest average pass rates under GPT-5.2 (39.2%), Qwen3.5 (50.3%), and Human evaluation (44.9%), surpassing open-source models such as OmniGen2, Kontext, and Qwen-IE-2509. When evaluated by Gemini-3, a commercial model (Nano Banana) showed a slightly higher pass rate (56.0% vs. FlowInOne's 54.0%), which the authors attribute to potential alignment bias given shared lineage. Despite this, FlowInOne's consistent performance across other neutral evaluators suggests its robustness.
*   **Quantitative Metrics**: Beyond pass rates, FlowInOne also exhibited strong quantitative performance.
    *   It achieved the highest average DINOv3 Sim score of 48.7% (compared to Nano Banana's 47.3%), indicating superior fine-grained spatial and physical control, particularly in tasks involving force and trajectory understanding, and text bounding box editing.
    *   In overall visual realism (CLIP-IQA) and semantic alignment (CLIP Score), FlowInOne performed comparably to commercial models and significantly outperformed open-source baselines. Its average CLIP Score was 0.283, and average CLIP-IQA was 0.684.

**5.2. Qualitative Comparison**
Qualitative examples illustrate that open-source models often struggle to differentiate visual instructions from image content, leading to artifacts or disregard for editing intentions. Commercial models like Nano Banana exhibited shortcomings in tasks requiring precise spatial and physical reasoning, such such as failing to translate visual markers into plausible physical dynamics or ignoring size constraints during bounding box insertions. In contrast, FlowInOne demonstrated a consistent ability to adhere to precise visual and textual constraints across various tasks, from basic editing to physics-aware generation.

**5.3. Ablation Studies**
A series of ablation studies were conducted to validate the architectural and training choices:
*   **Compression Method**: Comparing three strategies for aligning sequence length and feature dimensions from the visual encoder to the latent space, the **MLP + MLP** projection method yielded the best average pass rate (18.2%). This suggests that projecting both dimensions via MLPs effectively preserves semantic and spatial structure.
*   **Modulation Method**: Investigating mechanisms to unify generation and editing, the proposed **Dual-Path Spatially-Adaptive Modulation (SAM)** mechanism achieved the highest average pass rate (23.1%). This indicates that the adaptive gating mechanism effectively balances content consistency from the source image with adherence to instruction. Approaches lacking cross-attention performed significantly worse.
*   **Training Strategy**: Comparing two-stage training with joint training, **joint training** on all 5 million samples significantly outperformed the two-stage approach (47.8% vs. 29.1% average pass rate). This suggests that learning diverse tasks simultaneously within a shared visual flow space leads to better generalization and instruction adherence, avoiding catastrophic forgetting.
*   **Hyperparameter Ablation**: Optimal performance was observed with a CFG (Classifier-Free Guidance) scale of 7 and 50 sampling steps. For loss weights, setting the CLIP semantic alignment weight ($\beta_2$) to 1 and the KL divergence penalty ($\beta_1$) to 0.01 yielded the best results, highlighting the importance of balanced semantic alignment and latent space regularization.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

The FlowInOne framework introduces a notable shift in the paradigm of multimodal generation, proposing a fully vision-centric approach that unifies diverse generative tasks. The primary significance of this work lies in its reformulation of multimodal generation as a purely visual flow, where all input modalities are converted into visual prompts processed by a single "image-in, image-out" flow matching model.

This vision-centric approach carries several potential impacts:
*   **Architectural Simplification**: By eliminating the need for separate text encoders, cross-modal alignment modules, noise scheduling complexities, and task-specific architectural branches, FlowInOne offers a streamlined and more consistent architecture for multimodal generation. This simplification could lead to more efficient model development and deployment.
*   **Enhanced Spatial Precision and Instruction Adherence**: The direct embedding of textual and spatial instructions into the visual canvas, coupled with the Dual-Path Spatially-Adaptive Modulation, enables the model to achieve high levels of spatial precision and fidelity to instructions. This addresses a known limitation of traditional text-driven models in executing fine-grained, spatially explicit generation and editing tasks.
*   **Unified Interaction Interface**: The "image-in, image-out" paradigm provides a consistent and intuitive interface for users, allowing them to interact with a single model for a wide array of tasks, from basic text-to-image generation to complex layout-guided editing and physics-aware instruction following.
*   **Advancement in Generative Modeling**: By demonstrating the effectiveness of Flow Matching for continuous visual evolution within a shared latent space, the research contributes to the broader understanding and application of flow-based generative models, potentially paving the way for more stable and efficient generation processes compared to certain diffusion-based counterparts.
*   **New Resources for Research**: The introduction of the large-scale VisPrompt-5M dataset and the rigorous VP-Bench benchmark provides valuable resources for the research community. These datasets and benchmarks are specifically designed to push the boundaries of vision-centric instruction following, encouraging further development and robust evaluation in this emerging field.
*   **Bridging Perception and Creation**: The framework's ability to reason and generate entirely within the visual space suggests a step towards more holistic AI systems that can integrate visual understanding and creation under a single, coherent principle, thereby potentially closing the gap between visual perception and generation.

Overall, FlowInOne establishes a foundation for future vision-centric multimodal models, potentially influencing the design of more robust, unified, and spatially aware generative AI systems.
overview:
## The Vision-Centric Paradigm Shift in Multimodal Generation

Multimodal generation has traditionally operated under a text-dominant paradigm. In standard text-to-image systems, language serves as the primary control signal, with visual models acting as secondary translators that map textual embeddings into pixel space. This architecture creates an inherent asymmetry: while language can govern visual generation, the visual modality itself is rarely structured to reason or generate independently. Consequently, tasks that require precise spatial control—such as image editing based on doodles, bounding boxes, or physical trajectories—often require fragmented, task-specific modules or complex cross-modal alignment branches.

FlowInOne addresses this fragmentation by reformulating multimodal generation as a purely vision-centric "image-in, image-out" process. Instead of treating text and images as fundamentally different data types that must be aligned, this framework converts all inputs—including textual instructions, spatial layouts, and editing cues—into a single visual representation. By doing so, it leverages the inherent expressiveness of the visual modality to handle both understanding and generation within a unified latent space.

![Figure 2: Comparison of traditional text-to-image and text-image-to-image paradigms with the FlowInOne image-in, image-out approach.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.06757v1/x2.png)

This shift moves multimodal AI toward a more cohesive architecture. Rather than building a "Swiss Army knife" of modality-specific encoders, FlowInOne treats the generative process as a continuous visual flow. This approach aims to eliminate the bottlenecks associated with cross-modal alignment and the architectural complexities of diffusion-based denoising schedules.

## Unified Visual Semantics: Turning Text into Pixels

The core innovation of FlowInOne is the transformation of discrete language and spatial markers into a continuous visual semantic space. To achieve this, the system renders all instructions—be they text descriptions, bounding boxes, or directional arrows—directly onto a source image canvas, $I_v$. This rendering preserves the spatial context and structural priors of the input instruction relative to the image.

For example, if a user wants to "add a red hat" to a person in an image, they do not just provide the text; the text is placed on the canvas at the relevant location. If they provide a bounding box, it is drawn on the same canvas. This "visual prompt" is then processed by a Janus-Pro-1B visual encoder, specifically a SigLIP Vision Transformer, which extracts patch-level semantic features. These features are projected into a target embedding space $X_{fuse} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times D}$, capturing both the semantic meaning of the words and the geometric constraints of the visual markers.

![Figure 14: The data generation pipeline showing how diverse inputs like text, bounding boxes, and physical vectors are unified into a visual prompt canvas.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.06757v1/x14.png)

By representing instructions as pixels, the model avoids the modality gap that often plagues text-to-image models. The visual modality becomes the foundational language for both perception and creation. This unification allows the model to treat text-to-image generation, image editing, and even physics-based trajectory following as instances of the same fundamental task: evolving an input visual state into a target output state.

## Generative Visual Flow: Modeling Continuity

FlowInOne employs Flow Matching (FM) rather than the more common diffusion-based denoising. Flow matching treats generation as the problem of learning a continuous transport map between two distributions: a source distribution $p_0$ (representing the input visual prompt) and a target distribution $p_1$ (representing the desired output image).

In this framework, the model learns a velocity field $v_{\theta}$ that dictates how to transform a source latent state $z_0$ into a target latent state $z_1$ over a time interval $t \in [0, 1]$. The probability path between these states is defined as:

$$
z_t = tz_1 + (1 - (1 - \sigma_{\min})t) z_0
$$

The network is trained to predict the ground-truth velocity $v^{\star}_t$, which is the direct vector from the source to the target:

$$
v^{\star}_t = z_1 - (1 - \sigma_{\min})z_0
$$

Unlike diffusion models, which rely on Gaussian noise as a starting point and require complex noise schedules to manage the denoising process, Flow Matching allows the source distribution $z_0$ to be non-Gaussian. In FlowInOne, $z_0$ is the latent representation of the visual instruction itself. Inference then becomes a matter of solving an Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE) to transport the visual instruction's latent state to the target image's latent state. This deterministic principle provides a more stable and efficient bridge between the perception of an instruction and the generation of the corresponding content.

## The FlowInOne Architecture: Dual-Path Modulation

To handle the variety of generative tasks—ranging from creating images from scratch to modifying existing ones—FlowInOne uses a DiT (Diffusion Transformer) variant enhanced with a **Dual-Path Spatially-Adaptive Modulation (SAM)** mechanism. This architectural choice is critical for balancing two often-conflicting goals: adhering to new instructions and preserving the structural integrity of the original source image.

![Figure 4: The FlowInOne architecture featuring the DiT variant backbone and the Dual-Path Spatially-Adaptive Modulation mechanism.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.06757v1/x4.png)

The SAM mechanism operates differently depending on the task. A binary task indicator $I_{edit}$ determines the processing path:

1.  **Text-to-Image Generation ($I_{edit} = 0$):** When the model generates an image from text alone, the cross-attention layer is bypassed. This ensures the model focuses entirely on the semantic evolution from the visual prompt to the target image without being biased by external structural priors.
2.  **Image Editing ($I_{edit} = 1$):** When editing a source image $I_{src}$, the model must maintain the background and non-edited regions. The source image is encoded into a reference sequence $S$, and a cross-attention mechanism calculates a structural increment $\Delta H_{struct}$.

To prevent the source image's structure from "leaking" into areas that need to be completely changed (like replacing an object), an **Adaptive Gating Network** predicts a token-level weight vector $\Lambda \in [0, 1]^N$. This gating mechanism allows the model to selectively inject source priors only where they are needed, such as in the background, while allowing the instruction-driven generation to dominate in the edited regions. The final hidden state $H^{(l)}_{out}$ for a transformer layer is integrated as:

$$
H^{(l)}_{out} = \tilde{H}^{(l)} + I_{edit} \cdot (\Lambda \odot \Delta H_{struct})
$$

Where $\tilde{H}^{(l)}$ is the self-attention updated state. This design ensures that FlowInOne can perform precise local edits without sacrificing the realism of the global scene.

## Scaling Vision-Centric Instructions: VisPrompt-5M

To train a model capable of such diverse tasks, the researchers compiled **VisPrompt-5M**, a large-scale dataset of 5 million visual prompt pairs. This dataset moves beyond simple text-image pairs to include eight distinct task types that test different aspects of visual reasoning and generation:

*   **Standard Tasks**: Text-to-image and class-to-image generation.
*   **Editing Tasks**: Text-in-image editing, bounding box control, and "doodle" editing.
*   **Advanced Reasoning**: Visual marker editing (using arrows to indicate changes), trajectory understanding (predicting movement), and force understanding (simulating physical dynamics like wind or impact).

![Figure 3: Overview of the diverse task types and data distribution within the VisPrompt-5M dataset.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.06757v1/x3.png)

The inclusion of physics-aware tasks like force and trajectory understanding is a unique aspect of this work. By training on these samples, the model learns to interpret visual markers not just as static shapes, but as indicators of physical intent. For example, an arrow pointing at a ball can be interpreted as a force vector, prompting the model to generate the ball's resulting position and state in the target image.

## Evaluation and Performance: The VP-Bench Benchmark

Evaluating a unified multimodal model requires more than standard metrics like CLIP score, which often fail to capture spatial precision or instruction faithfulness. To address this, the researchers introduced **VP-Bench**, a benchmark of 1,060 curated image pairs evaluated across four dimensions: Instruction Faithfulness, Content Consistency, Visual Realism, and Spatial Precision.

![Figure 5: Comparison of FlowInOne against open-source and commercial models across various unified generation and editing tasks.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.06757v1/x5.png)

In head-to-head comparisons, FlowInOne demonstrated superior performance over existing open-source models like OmniGen2 and Kontext. Evaluation by large multimodal models (such as GPT-5.2 and Qwen3.5) and human judges showed that FlowInOne achieved the highest average "pass rates"—a metric requiring a successful score across all four evaluation dimensions. Specifically, it reached a 39.2% pass rate under GPT-5.2 evaluation, significantly higher than the 13.9% achieved by OmniGen2.

A key finding from the performance analysis was the model's ability to handle fine-grained spatial control. While commercial models sometimes ignored the precise dimensions of a bounding box or the direction of a visual marker, FlowInOne consistently adhered to these visual constraints. This success is attributed to the combination of the visual prompt rendering strategy and the adaptive modulation in the transformer blocks.

## Implications for Unified Multimodal AI

FlowInOne represents a step toward simplifying the architecture of multimodal generative models. By demonstrating that diverse tasks—from simple generation to complex physical simulation—can be unified as a single visual flow, the work challenges the necessity of complex, modality-specific subsystems. The "image-in, image-out" paradigm provides a consistent interface for both the model and the user, where the visual modality acts as the universal medium for interaction.

The success of the Flow Matching approach in this context suggests that generative modeling is moving toward more continuous, transport-based formulations. As models grow in scale, the ability to treat instructions as a starting point for a visual transport process, rather than a conditional signal for a denoising process, may prove to be a more robust way to achieve high-fidelity, instruction-faithful generation. Through the release of VisPrompt-5M and VP-Bench, this work provides the community with the resources to further explore the potential of vision-centric multimodal AI.

keyInsights: A single vision-centric flow matching model can effectively unify diverse generative tasks, such as text-to-image and image editing, by operating entirely within the visual space without relying on text encoders or task-specific architectures.

Converting all input modalities into visual prompts directly onto an image canvas effectively bridges the modality gap between discrete language and continuous visual data while preserving spatial layouts and structural priors.

The Dual-Path Spatially-Adaptive Modulation mechanism is crucial for dynamically injecting source image priors and selectively preserving background regions during editing, ensuring high instruction faithfulness and content consistency.
originalProblem: Multimodal generation often relies on text-dominant paradigms, leading to an asymmetry where language dictates visual output and vision lacks autonomous reasoning capabilities.
Unifying diverse tasks like visual understanding, editing, and generation within a single model architecture is challenging due to cross-modal alignment complexities and fragmented approaches.
Traditional generative models, such as diffusion models, often involve complex noise schedules and can face inefficiencies in sampling and optimization stability.
results: Achieved the highest average pass rates (39.2% by GPT-5.2, 50.3% by Qwen3.5, and 44.9% by human evaluators) compared to open-source models, and competitive performance against commercial models.

Demonstrated superior fine-grained spatial and physical control with the highest DINOv3 Sim score of 48.7%, particularly in tasks involving force/trajectory understanding and text bounding box editing.

Ablation studies validated the efficacy of the MLP+MLP projection, Dual-Path Spatially-Adaptive Modulation (SAM), and joint training strategy, confirming their essential roles in the model's overall performance.
solution: Reformulates multimodal generation as a purely visual "image-in, image-out" flow matching process, where all input modalities (text, spatial layouts, editing instructions) are converted into visual representations.
Develops a Flow Matching model augmented with a Dual-Path Spatially-Adaptive Modulation mechanism to dynamically balance structural preservation and instruction adherence during generation.
Creates a large-scale VisPrompt-5M dataset with 5 million visual prompt pairs and a curated VP-Bench benchmark for training and rigorously evaluating the model in a unified visual paradigm.
summary.summary: FlowInOne unifies multimodal generation tasks by converting diverse inputs into visual prompts within an "image-in, image-out" flow matching framework. This vision-centric approach achieves higher average pass rates and DINOv3 Sim scores compared to open-source models, demonstrating robust instruction adherence and spatial precision across text-to-image generation and image editing tasks.

title: FlowInOne:Unifying Multimodal Generation as Image-in, Image-out Flow Matching
